headline: ESF Proposes 4.1% Average Fee Rise as Subsidy Phase-Out Continues
dek: Hong Kong's largest English-medium school group has put forward another above-inflation fee increase for 2026–27, driven by the continuing withdrawal of government funding that once kept ESF costs well below the private sector.
author: marcus-chen
heroAlt: ESF Proposes 4.1% Average Fee Rise as Subsidy Phase-Out Continues

body.text: The English Schools Foundation, which operates 22 international schools across Hong Kong serving more than 18,000 students, has proposed an average fee increase of 4.1 per cent for the 2026–27 academic year. The plan, reported by the South China Morning Post in late March, would see ESF kindergartens raise fees by 5.8 per cent, while Discovery College — the group's private independent school on Lantau — is seeking a rise of 4.8 per cent, bringing secondary fees there to as high as HK$22,560 per month for Years 12 and 13.

The proposed increases are subject to approval by the Education Bureau, which regulates fee changes across Hong Kong's international school sector. All fees are collected in ten monthly instalments, a structure that has made ESF's headline annual costs appear lower than those of fully private operators — though the gap has narrowed considerably in recent years.

The structural driver behind ESF's recurring increases is well understood: a HK$283 million annual government subvention is being phased out over 13 years, a process that began nearly a decade ago. Subsidies for Years 1 through 7 have already ended entirely, shifting the cost directly onto fees. ESF has consistently framed its increases as necessary to maintain teaching quality — foundation chief executive Belinda Greer made that argument directly in a letter to parents accompanying this year's proposal.

For context, annual tuition at ESF primary and secondary schools currently ranges from HKD 93,700 to HKD 203,400 depending on year group, placing the group notably below the fully private international schools with which it most directly competes. ESF has maintained it remains "among the lowest" of international schools in Hong Kong, a claim that carries more weight in the lower secondary years than at sixth-form level.

The practical effect for existing families is modest in absolute terms — roughly HK$650 to HK$750 per month more at most school levels under the proposed structure. The more consequential question is trajectory. With the subsidy phase-out still several years from completion, families who enrolled when ESF was significantly cheaper than the private sector should model further annual increases in the 4 to 5 per cent range through to the early 2030s.

Separately, ESF has confirmed that from 1 September 2026 it will introduce a revised application fee of HK$1,500 for K1 and K2 applicants, pending final EDB approval, replacing the previous structure. The group is also retiring its Class B debenture across most kindergartens in 2026, replacing it with a tuition deposit — a simplification that admissions advisers say reduces upfront entry costs for some families even as annual fees climb.
